steps.title: Compute first-year earnings for each account
steps.content:
Account A earns 6% annual interest on \$500:
$$0.06 \times 500 = \$30$$
Account B earns a flat \$25 per year. So in year 1, Account A earns \$30 and Account B earns \$25.

steps.title: Track earnings in later years
steps.content:
Account A compounds annually, so its balance grows each year and so do its earnings:
Year 2 balance = \$530, earnings = $$0.06 \times 530 = \$31.80$$
Year 3 balance = \$561.80, earnings = $$0.06 \times 561.80 \approx \$33.71$$
Account B keeps earning a fixed \$25 every year. Since A starts above \$25 and only grows, A is always greater than B.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• A) Account A always earns more per year: A starts at \$30 and grows; B stays at \$25. True.
  • B) Account A always earns less per year: False — even in year 1, A earns \$30 vs. B's \$25.
  • C) A earns more at first but eventually earns less: A's earnings only grow, so it never drops below \$25. False.
  • D) A earns less at first but eventually more: A is already higher in year 1. False.
That matches choice A — A is correct.
